records.ety: [Cf. Dan. dukke, Sw. docka, OHG. doccha, G. docke. Cf. Doxy.]
[D. doek cloth, canvas, or Icel. dūkr cloth; akin to OHG. tuoh, G. tuch, Sw. duk, Dan. dug.]

defs: A linen (or sometimes cotton) fabric, finer and lighter than canvas, -- used for the lighter sails of vessels, the sacking of beds, and sometimes for men's clothing.
The light clothes worn by sailors in hot climates.

records.ety: [OE. duken, douken, to dive; akin to D. duiken, OHG. t<?/hhan, MHG. tucken, tücken, t<?/chen, G. tuchen. Cf. 5th Duck.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Ducked (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Ducking.]

defs: To thrust or plunge under water or other liquid and suddenly withdraw.
quotes: Adams, after ducking the squire twice or thrice, leaped out of the tub.

defs: To plunge the head of under water, immediately withdrawing it; as, duck the boy.
To bow; to bob down; to move quickly with a downward motion.
To go under the surface of water and immediately reappear; to dive; to plunge the head in water or other liquid; to dip.
quotes: In Tiber ducking thrice by break of day.

defs: To drop the head or person suddenly; to bow.
quotes: The learned pate Ducks to the golden fool.

records.ety: [OE. duke, doke. See Duck, v. t. ]

defs: Any bird of the subfamily Anatinæ, family Anatidæ.
notes: ☞ The genera and species are numerous. They are divided into river ducks and sea ducks. Among the former are the common domestic duck (Anas boschas); the wood duck (Aix sponsa); the beautiful mandarin duck of China (Dendronessa galeriliculata); the Muscovy duck, originally of South America (Cairina moschata). Among the sea ducks are the eider, canvasback, scoter, etc.

defs: A sudden inclination of the bead or dropping of the person, resembling the motion of a duck in water.
quotes: Here be, without duck or nod, Other trippings to be trod.
